lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: We have mastered the connectives. But there is a deeper pattern: a proof that works for one atom works for **any** atom.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Substitution. If you proved a theorem using atom $A$, you can substitute any formula for $A$ everywhere, and the proof still holds.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: `(subst N ...)` — give it a step and a mapping of atoms to formulas, and it rewrites the proof with those replacements. A single proof becomes a family of theorems.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: This is what makes logic a tool, not just a game. A proof about $A$ is really a proof about everything that fits the same shape.
